title: SEGMENT 2 — The tumor escape problem, in detail

lines.text: SEGMENT 2 — The tumor escape problem, in detail
Before we get to Allison and Honjo, we need to spend a little time on the problem they were solving.
Because if you don't really feel, in your bones, why tumors are so hard for the immune system to clear, the elegance of what these two men figured out won't land.
So here we go.
Your T cells, particularly the CD8 killer T cells, recognize their targets through a system called MHC class I.
Every cell in your body — every single one, with a few exceptions — displays, on its surface, a constant slideshow of tiny peptide fragments.
These fragments are chopped-up samples of whatever proteins the cell is currently making, inside.
They're loaded onto a molecule called MHC class I, which carries them out to the cell surface and holds them up for inspection.
T cells walk past, sniff the displays, and ask: do any of these peptides look foreign?
Do any of them look mutated?
Do any of them look like something I have been trained to attack?

If the answer is yes — if a virus is replicating inside the cell, or a protein has been mutated, or a foreign sequence has appeared — then a T cell that recognizes that peptide locks on, and within minutes the cell is dead.
This is the central surveillance loop of cellular immunity, and it runs continuously, in every tissue, all the time.
Now think about cancer in this context.
A cancer cell, by definition, carries mutations.
Many of those mutations land in proteins.
Those mutated proteins get chopped up like any other protein and displayed on MHC class I.
The peptides that result are called neoantigens — new antigens, sequences that don't exist in any normal tissue, sequences your immune system has never seen and was never trained to ignore.
A neoantigen, in principle, is a flashing red light.
It is exactly the kind of signal a T cell should pounce on.
And yet the cancer grows.
Why?
There are, broadly, four answers.
And these four answers map onto four ways tumors evade immunity, and onto four reasons immunotherapy sometimes works miraculously and sometimes doesn't work at all.
I want you to hold them in mind.
One.
The tumor doesn't make enough neoantigens to be visible.
Some cancers — pediatric leukemias, some sarcomas — have very few mutations.
They look, biochemically, almost normal.
Their MHC slideshow is boring.
T cells walk past and find nothing to attack.
Two.
The tumor stops displaying MHC class I altogether.
Some cancers mutate or downregulate the MHC machinery, so the slideshow goes dark.
The cell becomes invisible to T cells.
(Now, your body has a backup system for this — natural killer cells specifically attack cells that have stopped showing MHC — but tumors find ways around that too.)
Three.
The tumor surrounds itself with suppressive cells.

It recruits regulatory T cells, myeloid-derived suppressor cells, tumor-associated macrophages — a whole ecosystem of immune-dampening neighbors that turn the tumor microenvironment into a place where killer T cells get sluggish and demoralized.
It is, in effect, building a fortress out of corrupted immune cells.
Four — and this is the one that the Nobel-winning therapies attack.
The tumor activates the immune system's own brakes.
It displays, on its surface, ligands — protein molecules — that bind to the checkpoint receptors on T cells and tell those T cells: stand down.
PD-L1, the ligand for PD-1, is the famous example.
Many tumors learn to plaster themselves with PD-L1, in effect waving a white flag at every approaching T cell.
The T cell sees the flag, the PD-1 receptor on its surface engages, the killing program shuts off, and the cancer cell survives.
That fourth strategy is what we are going to spend the rest of this lecture dismantling.
Because once you understand that the cancer is waving white flags, the therapeutic strategy becomes obvious.
Take away the white flags.
Or blindfold the T cell so it can't see them.
That's a checkpoint inhibitor.
That's the whole game.
One more piece of intuition before we move on.

The reason this strategy works at all — the reason you can sometimes give a single drug, a single antibody, and watch a metastatic melanoma melt away over months — is that the T cells were already there.
They had already infiltrated the tumor.
They had already recognized the neoantigens.
They had already started their assault.
The cancer was suppressing them, but the army was on the field.
All the drug has to do is lift the suppression.
The killing was paused, not absent.
Hit the unpause button, and the killing resumes.
This is also why checkpoint inhibitors don't work on every cancer.
If the T cells never showed up to the tumor in the first place — if it's a so-called "cold" tumor, with no immune infiltrate — then there's nothing to unpause.
Releasing the brake on an empty road does nothing.
We'll come back to that problem in the second half.
For now, hold this picture.
The army is there.
It has been told to stand down.
The drug tells it to fight again.
